subject: Physics
q: A particle performs simple harmonic motion with amplitude A. Its speed is tripled at the instant that it is at a distance <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> from equilibrium position. The new amplitude of the motion is:
question: A particle performs simple harmonic motion with amplitude A. Its speed is tripled at the instant that it is at a distance <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> from equilibrium position. The new amplitude of the motion is:
solution:
For Original SHM,

using        v=ωA2-X2  

                v=ωA2-4A29

                =ω5A3

New SHM will be,

3v = ωAN2-XN2 

3ω5A3=ωAN2-4A29

5A2=AN2-4A29

AN2=49A29

AN=7A3

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical plastic bottle of negligible mass is filled with <math><mn>310</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>m</mi><mi>l</mi></math> of water and left floating in a pond with still water. If pressed downward slightly and released, it starts performing simple harmonic motion at angular frequency <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the radius of the bottle is <math><mn>2.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is close to: <math><mo>(</mo></math> density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow><mo>)</mo></math>
question: A cylindrical plastic bottle of negligible mass is filled with <math><mn>310</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>m</mi><mi>l</mi></math> of water and left floating in a pond with still water. If pressed downward slightly and released, it starts performing simple harmonic motion at angular frequency <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the radius of the bottle is <math><mn>2.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> then <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is close to: <math><mo>(</mo></math> density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
Let at equilibrium, y length of bottle is immerged in water:

So, mg=fbuo

mg=ρAyg ..... (1) [Equilibrium Condition]

Now it is further pushed by distance x

Frestoring=ρ[Ax+y)g-mg

mω2x=ρAxg+ρAyg-mg

ω=ρAgm=ρAgρV=πr2gV=7.9 rad/sec

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> attached to a horizontal spiral spring executes simple harmonic motion of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The maximum force exerted by spring on block is ___ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.
question: A rectangular block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> attached to a horizontal spiral spring executes simple harmonic motion of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The maximum force exerted by spring on block is ___ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.
solution:
By Newton's second law,

F=mamax.

The maximum acceleration for a SHM is given by 

amax=ω2A   ...(i)

The time period is given by 

T=2πω=3.14 s

⇒ω=2πT=2π3.14=2 rad s-1

Thus, the maximum acceleration from equation (i)

amax=4×1=4 m s-2

The maximum force is 

F=5 kg×4 m s-2=20 N

subject: Physics
q: Time period of a simple pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> inside a lift when the lift is stationary. If the lift moves upwards with an acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>g</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, the time period of pendulum will be :
question: Time period of a simple pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> inside a lift when the lift is stationary. If the lift moves upwards with an acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>g</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>, the time period of pendulum will be :
solution:
When lift is stationary 

T=2πLg

When lift is moving upwards

⇒ Pseudo force acts downwards

⇒geff=g+g2=3g2

⇒ New time period

T'=2πLgeff=2π2L3g

T'=23T

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> attached to a spring is made to oscillate with an initial amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> minutes the amplitude decreases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. Determine the value of the damping constant for this motion. (take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>693</mn></math> )
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> attached to a spring is made to oscillate with an initial amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> minutes the amplitude decreases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. Determine the value of the damping constant for this motion. (take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>693</mn></math> )
solution:
The amplitude of damped oscillation with time t and damping constant b is given by,

A=A0e-bt/2m

⇒lnA0A=bt2m

⇒ln2=b2m×120; here t=2 min=120 s

⇒0.693×2×1120=b

⇒b=1.16×10-2 kg s-1.

subject: Physics
q: A pendulum is executing simple harmonic motion and its maximum kinetic energy is $\mathrm{K}_{1}$. If the length of the pendulum is doubled and it performs simple harmonic motion with the same amplitude as in the first case, its maximum kinetic energy is $\mathrm{K}_{2}$
question: A pendulum is executing simple harmonic motion and its maximum kinetic energy is $\mathrm{K}_{1}$. If the length of the pendulum is doubled and it performs simple harmonic motion with the same amplitude as in the first case, its maximum kinetic energy is $\mathrm{K}_{2}$
solution:
$K=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} x^{2}$

$\Rightarrow K_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2}$

A $=L \theta$

$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$

$\Rightarrow K=\frac{1}{2} m \cdot \frac{g}{L} \cdot L^{2} \theta^{2}$

$=\frac{1}{2} m g L \theta^{2}$

$\therefore \frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\frac{L}{2 L}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow K_{2}=2 K_{1}$

$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{K}_{1}}{\mathrm{~K}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2 \mathrm{~L}}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{2}=2 \mathrm{~K}_{1}$

subject: Physics
q: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, attached to a horizontal spring, executes SHM with an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When this mass passes through its mean position, then a smaller mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>124</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is placed over it and both masses move together with amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be _____ .
question: A mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, attached to a horizontal spring, executes SHM with an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When this mass passes through its mean position, then a smaller mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>124</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is placed over it and both masses move together with amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be _____ .
solution:
At mean position, the velocity of the particle in S.H.M. is maximum, so kinetic energy is KE=12kA2, where, k is spring constant.

We also know that, kinetic energy is related to momentum as KE=p22m.

Now, from both equations, we have 

12kA2=p22m     (Here, momentum is conserved)

⇒A1 A22=m2 m1=1024900

⇒A1 A2=3230=1615=1616-1

∴α=16

subject: Physics
q:
A uniform disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$ is free to oscillate about the axis $A$ as shown in the figure. For small oscillations the time period is ______.
($g$ is acceleration due to gravity)
question:
A uniform disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$ is free to oscillate about the axis $A$ as shown in the figure. For small oscillations the time period is ______.
($g$ is acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
From the given figure, the axis of rotation is a horizontal line passing through the point $A$ on the edge of the disc. The circular arrow around the axis indicates that the disc oscillates out of its plane, meaning the axis of rotation is a tangent to the disc in its own plane.

The moment of inertia of a uniform disc about its diameter is $I_{cm} = \dfrac{1}{4}MR^2$.

Using the parallel axis theorem, the moment of inertia of the disc about the given axis of rotation is:
$I = I_{cm} + MR^2 = \dfrac{1}{4}MR^2 + MR^2 = \dfrac{5}{4}MR^2$

The distance from the center of mass of the disc to the axis of rotation is $d = R$.

The time period of a physical pendulum is given by:
$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I}{Mgd}}$

Substituting the values of $I$ and $d$:
$T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{\dfrac{5}{4}MR^2}{MgR}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{5R}{4g}}$

Answer: $2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{5R}{4g}}$
